message.content: {"schemaVersion": "0.1.0", "decisionId": "pilot-e1-001-turn-2-player-1", "agentId": "tom-prompted-llm", "selectedActionId": "play-single-heart-4-copy1", "teamObjective": {"type": "suppress_opponent", "explanation": "The leading opponent (player 0) has only 2 cards left and is near finishing. The immediate team objective is to take control of the trick with the cheapest legal card, deny the opponent a free lead continuation, and disrupt their endgame timing."}, "partnerBelief": {"summary": "My partner (player 3) has 3 cards left and has not yet acted in this trick. They might hold a higher heart or a trump that could beat the 3 of hearts, but their exact cards are hidden. If I pass, partner could potentially take control; however, waiting also lets the opponent retain initiative. I therefore believe partner is better served by me taking the lead now and giving them a fresh trick to respond to, rather than relying on them to rescue a pass.", "confidence": 0.55, "evidence": ["1aadd1a3-a6ea-44a2-ad2b-18a41c835526"]}, "opponentBelief": {"summary": "Player 0 led a low heart 3 and has only 2 cards remaining, which strongly suggests they are shedding a weak card to get down to a finishing hand. Player 2 (the other opponent, with 3 cards) may hold covering cards or may be preparing to pass to let player 0 lead again. I infer the opponents are trying to let player 0 finish quickly, so ceding control by passing is risky.", "confidence": 0.65, "evidence": ["1aadd1a3-a6ea-44a2-ad2b-18a41c835526"]}, "actionRationale": {"primaryReason": "Playing the 4 of hearts is the minimum card that legally beats the 3 of hearts. It takes control away from the near-finishing opponent at the lowest possible cost, preserving the 9 and 10 of hearts for later plays while immediately disrupting the opponent's likely endgame plan.", "whyNotAlternatives": [{"actionId": "pass", "reason": "Passing lets the near-finishing opponent possibly win the trick and lead again, accelerating their finish. My partner might rescue, but relying on hidden partner cards is less reliable than taking control myself when I can do so cheaply."}, {"actionId": "play-single-heart-9-copy1", "reason": "The 9 of hearts beats the 3, but it spends a materially stronger card than necessary. Since the 4 already wins the trick, using the 9 now would weaken my remaining hand without gaining additional board control."}, {"actionId": "play-single-heart-10-copy1", "reason": "The 10 of hearts also wins, but it is my highest card and would be an inefficient use of strength against a low 3. Saving the 10 for a later high-pressure trick is more valuable."}]}, "riskAssessment": {"risks": ["Player 2 may be able to beat my 4 of hearts with a higher heart or a trump, in which case I lose control and spend a card for little gain.", "If my partner had a strong response, my intervention may reduce their chance to act, though this is hidden information.", "I reveal that I have heart length/cards, which may help opponents count the suit."], "mitigation": "By using the minimum beating card (the 4), I limit resource exposure. Even if player 2 beats it, I have not over-invested, and I still have 9 and 10 of hearts plus whatever draws occur in later rounds."}, "confidence": 0.72, "notes": "Decision grounded on public hand counts and the observed low lead from a near-finishing opponent; hidden card beliefs remain probabilistic."}

{"agentId":"tom-prompted-llm","decisionId":"pilot-e1-002-turn-1-player-0","selectedActionId":"play-single-diamond-3-copy1","reasoning":{"partnerIntent":{"belief":"Partner (player 2) holds only 2 cards, the lowest count at the table, so they are near finishing. They likely want low-pressure leads or cards they can beat to shed a card and potentially go out.","evidence":["6e87a342-9381-417b-ad4a-b135c5334933"]},"opponentIntent":{"belief":"Opponents have made no public plays and hold 3 cards each. They likely want to win control, trap the short-handed partner, or force our team to spend high cards.","evidence":["6e87a342-9381-417b-ad4a-b135c5334933"]},"counterfactuals":[{"actionId":"play-single-diamond-6-copy1","assessment":"Leading the 6 uses our strongest non-trump. It is more likely to win the trick, but it wastes the only high card in a 4-card hand and prevents the near-finish partner from beating the lead.","evidence":["6e87a342-9381-417b-ad4a-b135c5334933"]},{"actionId":"play-single-diamond-3-copy1","assessment":"Leading the 3 expends the lowest card, keeps all stronger cards for later, and maximizes the chance the partner can beat it to reduce their remaining hand.","evidence":["6e87a342-9381-417b-ad4a-b135c5334933"]}],"conclusion":"Because the partner is near finish and we have no trump cards, the lowest non-trump lead best supports the team objective while preserving stronger cards for future tricks."},"confidence":0.75}

{"actionId": "play-single-club-3-copy1", "agentId": "tom-prompted-llm", "decisionId": "pilot-e1-003-turn-1-player-0", "timestamp": "2026-06-18T16:14:07.002208+08:00", "selectedActionId": "play-single-club-3-copy1", "reasoning": {"playerIntent": {"goals": ["_opening_lead_low_to_keep_control", "_avoid_giving_partner_confusing_high_card"], "beliefs": {"partnerLikelyHasMixedHand": "Since no cards have been played, partner's hand is unknown; they hold 2 cards, a smaller count than opponents' 3/3 and our 4, suggesting they may be closer to going out or hold a specific short structure.", "opponentsHold3Each": "Both opponents have 3 cards, so the trick could be contested; leading low avoids immediately exposing our highest card."}, "evidence": ["3bf96ebc-a4bf-4049-9b24-01deafc42642"]}, "opponentIntent": {"threats": ["_opponent_may_cover_with_trump_or_high_card", "_opponent_near_finish_player_1_has_2_cards"], "countermeasures": ["_lead_lowest_single_to_force_opponents_to_spend_value", "_preserve_6_for_later_control"], "evidence": ["3bf96ebc-a4bf-4049-9b24-01deafc42642"]}, "counterfactuals": [{"actionId": "play-single-club-6-copy1", "comparison": "Leads the highest club. If an opponent lacks a higher non-trump club, this might win the trick, but it also gives up our best card early and leaves us with 3-5-4, all weaker.", "assessment": "Worse because it reduces future control and does not exploit the partner's position."}, {"actionId": "play-single-club-3-copy1", "comparison": "Leads the lowest club. This keeps 4-5-6 for later tricks and forces opponents to reveal strength to take the trick.", "assessment": "Better because it preserves hand strength and is the standard opening-lead heuristic in Guandan."}]}, "hiddenInformationNote": "Partner and opponent exact cards are unknown; inferences are probabilistic and based only on hand counts and the new_game event.", "confidence": 0.85}

{"schemaVersion": "0.1.0", "decisionId": "pilot-e1-004-turn-1-player-0", "agentId": "tom-prompted-llm", "selectedActionId": "play-single-spade-2-copy2", "teamObjective": {"type": "finish_hand", "explanation": "With only two cards left, the priority is to retain the lead and play out the hand before opponents can take control."}, "partnerBelief": {"summary": "Partner has three cards and no public signals yet; they likely hold at least one playable card but may not be able to assist if opponents seize the lead.", "confidence": 0.5, "evidence": ["27a50f10-d0ca-4a52-af45-cf0a09317796"]}, "opponentBelief": {"summary": "Opponents collectively hold nine cards, so some higher cards (trump 7s, jokers, or other 2s) are plausible, but exact holdings are unknown.", "confidence": 0.5, "evidence": ["27a50f10-d0ca-4a52-af45-cf0a09317796"]}, "actionRationale": {"primaryReason": "Leading the 2 first maximizes the chance of winning this trick outright because fewer cards beat a 2 than beat an Ace. If the 2 wins, the remaining Ace can be played next to finish the hand.", "whyNotAlternatives": [{"actionId": "play-single-spade-A-copy2", "reason": "Leading the Ace first risks losing the lead to any opponent holding a 2, joker, or trump 7. Since the 2 is already in hand, it is better used as the lead to remove higher cards from opponents before playing the Ace."}]}, "riskAssessment": {"risks": ["An opponent may hold a joker or trump 7 and beat the 2, leaving the Ace vulnerable to a later non-single lead.", "If the partner cannot cover, the lead may pass to opponents who can dictate the next trick's format."], "mitigation": "The 2 is the strongest lead available; winning this trick lets the agent control the next trick and play the Ace to empty the hand."}, "confidence": 0.65}

{"schemaVersion": "0.1.0", "decisionId": "pilot-e1-005-turn-1-player-0", "agentId": "tom-prompted-llm", "selectedActionId": "play-single-spade-3-copy1", "teamObjective": {"type": "transfer_lead_to_partner", "explanation": "With no trumps and only low spades, leading the weakest card invites partner to take control with a higher card while preserving my limited high cards for later."}, "partnerBelief": {"summary": "Partner has 3 unknown cards. Since no cards have been played, partner is roughly equally likely to hold higher spades, trump-rank cards, or other suits. I believe partner can plausibly beat a low spade lead but should not assume it.", "confidence": 0.5, "evidence": ["92c3571c-01b6-4559-bc9e-dba33200863e"]}, "opponentBelief": {"summary": "Each opponent holds 3 unknown cards. They likely contain cards higher than spade-3, and possibly trump-rank 7s or red trumps. They will probably overtake a low spade lead if they can, but they cannot reveal hidden cards yet.", "confidence": 0.6, "evidence": ["92c3571c-01b6-4559-bc9e-dba33200863e"]}, "actionRationale": {"primaryReason": "Leading the spade-3 is the minimal commitment: it is the lowest card in my hand, least likely to win outright, and keeps the 4/5/6 for future tricks or to avoid being forced to play them under pressure.", "whyNotAlternatives": [{"actionId": "play-single-spade-6-copy1", "reason": "Playing the 6 wastes my highest non-trump and still loses to almost any trump or higher card; it gives opponents more information without meaningful gain."}, {"actionId": "play-single-spade-4-copy1", "reason": "The 4 is only marginally stronger than the 3 but loses the same card-positioning advantage; keeping sequential low cards is more valuable."}, {"actionId": "play-single-spade-5-copy1", "reason": "The 5 sits in the middle of my hand and is better retained for a later lead or response than thrown away opening the trick."}]}, "riskAssessment": {"risks": ["An opponent may play a trump or higher spade and keep the lead, forcing my team onto defense.", "If partner is also weak, the opening 3 may simply pass control to opponents without learning much."], "mitigation": "By leading the lowest card I minimize resource loss regardless of outcome, leaving my slightly higher spades available for later tricks or to cover partner."}, "confidence": 0.75}

{"schemaVersion": "0.1.0", "decisionId": "pilot-e1-012-turn-1-player-0", "agentId": "tom-prompted-llm", "selectedActionId": "play-single-diamond-3-copy1", "teamObjective": {"type": "transfer_lead_to_partner", "explanation": "Partner (player 2) has only 2 cards remaining and is close to finishing. By leading the lowest available single, I maximize the chance that partner can overtake the trick and use their remaining cards efficiently, while minimizing the risk of handing a high-value lead to opponents."}, "partnerBelief": {"summary": "Partner likely holds 2 cards that could include a trump, a high non-trump, or a pair/bomb; they are probably trying to finish their hand quickly, so they may be willing to spend a moderately strong card to take the lead if I lead low.", "confidence": 0.55, "evidence": ["1c5e98c0-0f15-452f-b8a0-17e10364e58d"]}, "opponentBelief": {"summary": "Opponents have not acted, so their exact strength is unknown. With 3 cards each, they are unlikely to be as close to finishing as partner, but they may still hold trumps or high cards to capture a low lead. They will likely try to seize the lead to stall partner's finish.", "confidence": 0.5, "evidence": ["1c5e98c0-0f15-452f-b8a0-17e10364e58d"]}, "actionRationale": {"primaryReason": "Leading the 3 of diamonds is the weakest lead, giving partner the widest range of cards that can beat it and giving opponents the least value if they capture the trick. It also preserves my slightly higher diamonds for later.", "whyNotAlternatives": [{"actionId": "play-single-diamond-6-copy1", "reason": "Leading the highest card keeps some low cards but makes it harder for partner to overtake; if opponents pass I retain the lead with a weak hand, which does not help partner finish."}, {"actionId": "play-single-diamond-5-copy1", "reason": "The 5 is still a middling card; it may be beaten by opponents or partner, but it offers less transfer value than the 3 and saves little compared with leading the 3."}, {"actionId": "play-single-diamond-4-copy1", "reason": "The 4 is only slightly better than the 3 as a lead, but the 3 still maximizes partner's ability to win and minimizes the trick's value to opponents."}]}, "riskAssessment": {"risks": ["Any opponent with a higher non-trump or trump can take the trick immediately.", "Partner may not be able to beat the lead and could be forced to pass, leaving opponents in control.", "My remaining cards are all low non-trumps, so I cannot easily regain the lead."], "mitigation": "Leading the lowest card minimizes the strength opponents must spend to win and leaves my highest card for a later opportunity; if partner has a winning card, they are most likely able to use it on a 3 lead."}, "confidence": 0.6, "notes": "No cards have been played, so beliefs remain probabilistic. The hand-count signal that partner has only 2 cards is the main driver for a low lead."}

{"schemaVersion":"0.1.0","decisionId":"pilot-e1-015-turn-1-player-0","agentId":"tom-prompted-llm","selectedActionId":"play-single-spade-3-copy1","teamObjective":{"type":"save_resources","explanation":"Opening lead with only low non-trump spades.
